text: Prepared by the OAK RIDGE NATIONAL LABORATORY Oak Ridge, Tennessee 37830 operated by UNION CARBIDE CORPORATION for the Office of Industrial Programs U.S.
We studied the corrosion of Fe-Ni-Cr alloys by draw salt (60 wt $\%$ NaNO $_3$ -40 wt $\%$ KNO $_3$ ) with thermal convection loops of alloy 800 and types 304L and 316 stainless steel.
The main corrosion processes at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and below were the growth of thin oxide scales and the dissolution of chromium by the salt.
Spallation of oxide layers occurred on type 304 stainless steel specimens at intermediate temperatures.
Results indicated relatively low corrosion rates (<13 $\mu$ m/year in most cases) for temperatures of $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and less.
Corrosion of type 316 stainless steel was greatly accelerated when the maximum loop temperature was raised to $620^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
It therefore appears that $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ may be the limiting temperature for use of the above alloys in draw salt.

A mixture of 60 wt $\%$ $\mathrm{NaNO}_3 - 40$ wt $\%$ $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ (draw salt) has been proposed for use in solar thermal power systems as both a solar receiver working fluid and a thermal energy storage medium.

The subject experiments were therefore performed in closed-loop systems to study the corrosion of alloy 800 and types 304 and 316 stainless steel by slowly flowing $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ - $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ under an impressed temperature gradient.

Previous work with a nitrate-nitrite [49 $\mathrm{NaNO}_3 - 44$ $\mathrm{KNO}_3 - 7$ $\mathrm{NaNO}_2$ (wt %)] salt revealed unacceptably high corrosion rates of type 316 stainless steel at $550^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

The as-received draw salt, with a nominal composition of 60 wt $\%$ $\mathrm{NaNO}_3 - 40$ wt $\%$ $\mathrm{KNO}_3^*$ and a melting point of $221^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , was outgassed in a closed type 304 stainless steel vessel at 250 to $300^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ while simultaneously being purged by argon flowing above and through the salt.
Each of the three loops was constructed of different alloys: types 304L and 316 stainless steel and alloy 800 (Table 1).
Tubing for the loops ranged from 1.9 to $2.5\mathrm{cm}$ in outside diameter and from 0.2 to $0.3\mathrm{cm}$ in wall thickness.
Insert coupons were approximately $1.9\times 0.8\times 0.1\mathrm{cm}$ and were in the as-annealed condition (unpolished).
In each TCL, the pressure above the salt rapidly increased to above $6.9\times 10^{4}\mathrm{Pa}$ , gage (10 psig) when the temperature was initially raised above about
$520^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
During most of our study the three TCLs operated with maximum temperatures and temperature differentials $\Delta T_{\mathrm{s}}$ of about 600 and

The $\Delta T$ was established by first setting the minimum temperature at the bottom of the cold leg and then increasing the output of the heaters along the hot leg to achieve a maximum temperature of $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ just below the bottom of the hot-leg surge tank (Fig.
The nominal salt velocity was $4\mathrm{mm/s}$ .

After $5000\mathrm{h}$ of coupon exposure, the $\Delta T$ of the type 316 stainless steel TCL was changed to $150^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and its maximum temperature was maintained at $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ to assess any changes in behavior due to mass transfer effects (see below).
After 1500 and $4500\mathrm{h}$ of exposure, corners were clipped from the specimens in each loop for metallographic examination.

Total chromium and chromium(VI) concentrations in the salt were measured spectrophotometrically, $^{6,7}$ and nickel and molybdenum levels were determined by the atomic absorption spectroscopic graphite furnace technique.

The salt samples were analyzed for iron spectrophotometrically with the o-phenanthroline method. $^{8}$ The nitrate concentrations were determined by a modified DeVarda method; $^{9}$ the nitrite levels were measured volumetrically by titration with cerium(IV) (ref.

High-resolution electron microprobe analysis allowed determination of the chemical composition of the reaction zone of type 316 stainless steel after $4500\mathrm{h}$ of exposure at $595^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The salt in each of the TCLs was periodically analyzed to determine the extent and nature of corrosion product species; results are shown in Table 2.
Most probably the measurements are erroneous because we observed a decrease to a "normal" concentration in the alloy 800 loop at a later sampling time.
In all the loops, the salt's nitrite concentration increased rapidly to about 1.9 wt % but then increased more slowly.
As described in the experimental procedures section, the pressure above the loop salt rapidly increased when the temperature was raised above about $520^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Maximum loop temperature $T_{\text{max}}$ was about $625^{\circ}C$ ; all other measurement times were for a $T_{\text{max}}$ of about $600^{\circ}C$ .
A substantial amount of gaseous oxygen rapidly evolved when the draw salt in these closed loops was heated to above about $520^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Such a reaction was reported earlier for this salt.<sup>1</sup> As seen in Table 2, the measured nitrite concentrations in the salt increased in accordance with the decomposition reaction and are in approximate agreement with those calculated with the equilibrium constant for Eq.
11) and the assumption that a mass balance was maintained between nitrate and nitrite.
The weight changes reported in Tables A.1 through A.3 are not indicative of the true corrosion rates.

The measured weight change $\Delta W$ is actually the difference between the weight of the reacted oxygen $\Delta W_{0}$ and the sum of the weights of the dissolved elements (principally chromium) $\Delta W_{D}$ and of the spalled material $\Delta W_{6}$ :
\Delta W = \Delta W _ {O} - \left(\Delta W _ {D} + \Delta W _ {S}\right). \tag {2}
The weight of the oxygen acquired by a specimen $\Delta W_{0}$ can be roughly calculated from the thickness of the oxide layer $x$ (see Tables A.4-A.6):

(2) and (5), respectively, and appropriate measurements of $\Delta W$ and $x$ from Tables A.1 through A.6.

For our case, we will assume $\Delta W_{S} = 0$ because observation of possible spallation after 4500 h of exposure was restricted to a very few specimens in one loop (specimens H6 and H7 in the type 304L stainless steel TCL).
approximate values of the total weight of oxidized metal of each loop specimen are listed in Tables A.7 through A.12.

Corrosion weight changes and rates obtained in this manner for the alloy 800 and type 316 stainless steel loops (Tables A.7-A.10) are consistent with the expectation that oxidation kinetics, and therefore total weight loss, should increase with increasing temperature (this is not the case for the uncorrected weight changes measured with the oxide films intact).
In contrast, the weight losses calculated for specimens in the type 304L stainless steel loop after $4500\mathrm{h}$ of exposure did not maximize at the highest loop temperature.
In this case, relatively large corrosion rates resulted at intermediate temperatures (see Table A.12).

However, the actual corrosion rates at these positions are somewhat less than those calculated because the calculations assumed a $100\%$ dense oxide film of the same thickness as that of the observed porous film.

Regardless of possible inaccuracies in the determinations of $\Delta V_{T}$ , the weight change results shown in Tables A.7 through A.12 indicate that the metal in the oxide film constitutes most of the metal loss and that the total corrosion rates are relatively small.

Figure 16 shows that the oxide thicknesses on the alloys of this study are about an order of magnitude less than those expected for annealed type 304 stainless steel in steam.[12] The total weight losses after $4500\mathrm{h}$ in draw salt have been converted into corrosion rates in the last column of Tables A.8, A.10, and A.12 and indicate that, while the "worst case" corresponds to $25~{\mu\mathrm{m}}/$ year (1 mil/year), the corrosion rate is substantially less in most cases.

This would be consistent with the microprobe data and agrees with the results from a study of the corrosion of type 304 stainless steel by thermally convective draw salt.

Another complicating factor is the multitude of layers observed in the reaction zones of types 304 and 304L stainless steel when their polished cross sections are etched (Fig.

This variety of metallographically distinct structures was not observed in another study of type 304 stainless steel, and the reason why so many layers should develop on these alloys and not on the other materials
Finally, the micrographs of the higher temperature alloy 800 coupons indicated some tendency for internal oxidation at the reaction zone-base metal interface [Fig.
7(a)], although longer term tests will be required to establish the significance of this observation.
Because most of the chromium in the salt was in the +6 oxidation state (Table 2), $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{Cr}_2\mathrm{O}_7$ and $\mathrm{Na}_2\mathrm{CrO}_4$ are probable species.
Recent work<sup>3</sup> on the corrosion of type 304 stainless steel in draw salt revealed a time dependence of $t^{0.4}$ for $C_{\mathrm{Cr(s)}}$ .
In agreement with other work<sup>3,5</sup> our data generally indicate a continuing increase in chromium levels of the salt with time (Table 2) for the duration of the experiments.

The chromium concentration of the salt in the type 316 stainless steel TCL appeared to approach a time-independent value after several thousand hours and then increased again when the maximum loop temperature was increased.

In no case, however, was the time dependence of the chromium concentrations in the salt greater than $t^{0.5}$ for operation at a maximum temperature of $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ; the best-fit exponent was always between 0.1 and 0.5.

observed in other molten salt systems (principally hydroxides or fluorides) subjected to a thermal gradient. $^{13}$ The transport mechanism can involve oxidation and dissolution of reactive metals as corrosion products in the hot zone with subsequent reduction and deposition of the metals in the cold zone.
Furthermore, changes in the $\Delta T$ of the type 316 stainless steel loop did not significantly affect the measured weight changes (Fig.
Thus, our results are encouraging in that thermal gradient mass transport will not be a significant operational problem in draw salt systems up to $600^{\circ}C$ .

The results of this study indicate that alloy 800 and types 304, 304L, and 316 stainless steel have low corrosion rates in thermally convective $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ - $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ at a maximum temperature of $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Under such conditions, the greatest metal loss rate corresponded to $25~\mu \mathrm{m}$ /year, but in most cases the rate was less than $13~\mu \mathrm{m}$ /year.
These latter rates are somewhat smaller than those reported for similar loops operated with the salt exposed to the atmosphere<sup>3,5</sup> but are within a factor of 2 to 5.
Significant metal oxidation was observed only at intermediate temperatures in the type 304L stainless steel TCL after $5000\mathrm{h}$ of salt exposure (Fig.
6), but the overall rate did not exceed $25~\mu \mathrm{m}$ /year.
Raising the maximum loop temperature to $620^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ resulted in much greater corrosion rates for type 316 stainless steel (Fig.

This rapid acceleration of the corrosion process was also observed in other studies of types 304 and 316 stainless steel and alloy 800 in draw salt loops,<sup>3,5</sup> where at $630^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ significant spalling of the oxide films on type 316 stainless steel was observed.<sup>5</sup> Our results therefore confirm that $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ probably represents an upper temperature service limit for such alloys in $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ - $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ (ref.

Alloy 800 and types 304, 304L, and 316 stainless steel were exposed to thermally convective $\mathrm{NaNO}_3$ - $\mathrm{KNO}_3$ draw salt at 375 to $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for more than $4500\mathrm{h}$ .

The weight change data for the alloys indicated that (1) the metal in the oxide film constituted most of the metal loss; (2) the corrosion rate, in general, increased with temperature; and (3) although the greatest metal loss corresponded to a penetration rate of $25~{\mu\mathrm{m}}/$ year (1 mil/year), the rate was less than $13~{\mu\mathrm{m}}/$ year in most cases.
Raising the maximum temperature of the type 316 stainless steel loop from 595 to $620^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ dramatically increased the corrosion rate.
It thus appeared that $600^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ may be the limiting temperature for use of such alloys in draw salt.
